figures.caption: Figure 1: Schematic of the proposed validation-driven clustering framework for adaptive pooling under predictive heterogeneity.

Figure 12: Single-series full-day prediction comparison at horizon h = 3. The dashed vertical lines indicate the validation and test split points, and shaded regions correspond to the validation and test segments. The forecasts from Cluster(Ours) more closely follow the held-out future trajectory across the selected channels.
Table 4: PEMS-SF TEST results (scaled by ×100) for horizons h ∈ {1, 3, 6}. Bold indicates the selected K⋆ for each method.
Table 5: Validation selection statistics across random seeds for the feature-based K-means baseline Feat-Kmeans on PEMS-BAY.
Table 6: Validation selection statistics across random seeds for the Random-Balanced baseline Random-Balanced on PEMS-BAY.
Figure 5: Distribution of per-series MSE improvement over GLOBAL at horizons h = 1, 3, 6.
Table 2: PEMS-BAY TEST results (scaled by ×100) for horizons h ∈ {1, 3, 6}. Bold indicates the selected K⋆ for each method.
Figure 10: Calibration and sharpness of probabilistic forecasts for the PEMS-SF data.
Figure 8: Probabilistic forecasting accuracy across prediction horizons h ∈ {1, 3, 6} using the selected K⋆ for the PEMS-SF data.
Figure 9: Practical impact of clustering-based forecasting in terms of benefit fraction and fallback usage for the PEMS-SF data.
Table 7: Validation selection statistics across random seeds for the proposed VAL-driven method on PEMS-BAY. Selection uses routed VAL Huber loss with penalty.
Figure 2: Probabilistic forecasting accuracy across prediction horizons h ∈ {1, 3, 6} using the selected K⋆ for the PEMS-BAY data.

Figure 11: Representative trajectory-level comparisons at horizon h = 3. Each subplot compares GLOBAL, Feat-Kmeans, Random-Balanced, and Cluster(Ours) against the true future trajectory for selected series–channel pairs. Cluster(Ours) generally remains closer to the held-out future trajectory, illustrating consistent trajectory-level improvements.
Figure 4: Calibration and sharpness of probabilistic forecasts for the PEMS-BAY data.
Figure 3: Practical impact of clustering-based forecasting in terms of benefit fraction and fallback usage for the PEMS-BAY data.

Figure 7: Single-series full-day prediction comparison at horizon h = 6 for selected channels of one representative series. The dashed vertical lines mark the VAL and TEST split points. The forecasts from Cluster(Ours) more closely track the held-out future segment in these selected channels, illustrating the trajectory-level improvements reflected in the aggregate error distributions.
Table 8: Validation selection statistics across random seeds for the feature-based K-means baseline Feat-Kmeans on PEMS-SF.
Table 9: Validation selection statistics across random seeds for the random balanced baseline Random-Balanced on PEMS-SF.
Table 10: Validation selection statistics across random seeds for the VAL-driven clustering method (Ours) on PEMS-SF.

Figure 6: Representative full-day prediction panels at horizon h = 6. Each subplot compares GLOBAL, Feat-Kmeans, Random-Balanced, and Cluster(Ours) against the true future trajectory for selected series-channel pairs. In these representative examples, Cluster(Ours) tends to remain closer to the held-out future trajectory.
